EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across the region causing strong winds over a good portion of the area from late morning into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured peak gust of 68 mph 4 miles NW of West Richland in Benton county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of rain fell along the west slopes of the Blue Mountains from February 1st through 4th.  The increased runoff caused high water levels and minor flooding along the Umatilla and Walla Walla Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walla Walla river near Touchet - Flood Stage 13 feet.  River rose to flood stage at 1500 PST, cresting at 13.0 feet at 1530 PST, then went below flood stage after 1600 PST.  Only brief minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across the region causing strong winds over a good portion of the area from late morning into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated peak gust of 63 mph at The Dalles municipal airport in Klickitat county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved across the region causing strong winds over a good portion of the area from late morning into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured peak gust of 60 mph 4 miles NE of Lyle in Klickitat county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold low pressure system brought snow levels down near sea level. Though temperatures at sea level were a bit too warm for much accumulating snow, up in the Cascade Foothills, snow accumulated quickly with amounts generally around 5 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports on Twitter of 9 inches of snow on Mt Brynion, 3 miles east of Kelso, and 6 inches of snow near Yacolt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system slid down the coast and stayed offshore, pulling cold air from east of the Cascades into southwest Washington. This brought snow levels down to sea level, and moisture from the low pressure system meant snow down to sea level around Vancouver and along the lower Columbia River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report via social media of 7.0 inches North of Battleground.  Another report via Facebook of 8.0 inches of snow  at 750 ft in Battleground. Elsewhere around the Vancouver area there were several reports of 4 to 6 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observer reported 8.0 inches of snow 9 miles north of Yacolt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist low pressure system brought heavy snow to the Cascades. Snow levels started around 1500 feet but rose to settle around 2500 feet for the rest of the event, with most of the snow falling above 2500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinto Rock SNOTEL recorded 14 inches of snow, and June Lake SNOTEL recorded 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of February 13th a surface low pressure formed over eastern Washington and tracked southeastward into central Idaho on February 14th before weakening. The western Columbia Basin received minor accumulations of snow which quickly melted during the day, however the rising terrain of the eastern basin including the Palouse region and the Spokane area as well as the northeastern mountains of Washington were north of the track and under the most intense precipitation from this storm. Widespread heavy snow accumulations resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Hunters reported 8.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of February 7th and into the early morning hours of February 8th strong winds down sloping off the Cascades in the wake of a frontal passage caused scattered power outages due to trees interacting with power lines in the Wenatchee and Entiat area. The highest recorded wind gust was 52 mph at the Wenatchee airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chelan County Public Utilities reported about 1500 customers lost power during the night of February 7th and morning of February 8th when strong winds broke tree limbs and downed a number of trees into power lines in and around Wenatchee and in the Entiat area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees closed Navarre Coulee Road for about three hours while road crews cleared the damaged trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays River near Rosburg crested at 12.6 feet around 1 AM, which is 0.6 foot above flood stage. No damage was reported.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system brought rain and snow the region during the second week of January. Snow levels were relatively high for this time of year, with snow accumulating above 2000 feet in the Washington Cascade east slopes and above 3800 feet in the Blue mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snow accumulation of 12 inches at Easton in Kittitas county.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific system brought snow to Kittitas county January 23rd and 24th.  Heavy snow accumulated over the western portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snow accumulation of 13 inches, 7 miles NNW of Easton in western Kittitas county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of January 23rd a warm front formed over eastern Washington and North Idaho fed by a deep and favorably directed fetch of Pacific moisture. The front became stationary and produced a 24 to 36 hour period of light to moderate snow impacting the mountains and valleys of the Cascades and northeastern Washington. The Okanogan Valley also received heavy snow from this storm system. The Columbia Basin was in the warm sector of this storm system and received mostly rain with 2 to 5 inches of wet snow noted in the far western basin near the Cascades and on the Waterville Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Boundary Dam reported 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

The front became stationary and produced a 24 to 36 hour period of light to moderate snow impacting the mountains and valleys of the Cascades and northeastern Washington. The Okanogan Valley also received heavy snow from this storm system. The Columbia Basin was in the warm sector of this storm system and received mostly rain with 2 to 5 inches of wet snow noted in the far western basin near the Cascades and on the Waterville Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 8 miles southeast of Northport reported 5.2 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system off the Washington coast formed a stationary front draped across eastern Washington during the day of January 11th. A second low pressure formed along this front and migrated through eastern Washington and north Idaho. A rich plume of Pacific moisture enhanced into heavy wet snow across the Cascades and eastern Washington. Outside of the Cascades the heaviest snow accumulations were noted just to the north of the track of the low pressure and was largely confined to a 50 mile wide area across the northern Columbia Basin and the southern portions of the northern Washington mountain zones. Peripheral areas near the Canadian border and over the southern Columbia basin received generally 2 to 4 inches of snow from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 12 miles south of Wauconda reported 4.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist warm front overran cold air trapped against the Cascades during the early morning hours of January 27th. Heavy snow accumulations in the Wenatchee area and on the Waterville Plateau resulted. Moderate accumulations of 2 to 4 inches of snow were noted in the Okanogan Valley as well as the mountains and valleys of northeast Washington. ||In the Cascades, the southern valleys south of Lake Chelan received heavy snow accumulations with 8 to 10 inches in the Wenatchee River and Entiat River Valleys. This heavy wet snow toppled trees and produced scattered power outages in the upper Wenatchee Valley. Areas north of Lake Chelan received moderate amounts of snow in the 4 to 6 inch range, which is below heavy snow criteria and not unusual for winter in these valleys.   ||A following cold front ended the precipitation by dawn in the Cascades, but then strong post frontal winds developed over the area. A wind sensor on Mission Peak at 6730 feet elevation recorded a westerly gust of 110 mph. Some of these high winds aloft descended down the east slopes of the Cascades and channeled through the Wenatchee Valley and produced damage in the Wenatchee area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Waterville reported 4.0 inches of new snow accumulation. The COOP observer at Chief Joseph Dam just north of the sparsely populated Waterville Plateau recorded 4.7 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist occluded front passed through eastern Washington on January 16th. During the early morning hours this front brought pockets of freezing rain to some areas of eastern Washington. Generally a few hundredths of an inch to slightly over one tenth of an inch of ice accumulation was noted in the Okanogan Valley and the southern valleys of the northeast Washington mountains. Mainly rain fell over the Columbia Basin with minor accumulations of snow in the mountains and the far northern valleys near the Canadian Border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remote observing station at the Omak Airport reported 0.15 inches of freezing rain accumulation in a six hour period from 2:00 AM to 6:00 AM before turning over to light snow.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought snow to portions of the Interior Northwest January 25th through 27th.  Heaviest snow was over portions of the Washington Cascade east slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured snow accumulation of 15.5 inches at Easton in western Kittitas county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system off the Washington coast formed a stationary front draped across eastern Washington during the day of January 11th. A second low pressure formed along this front and migrated through eastern Washington and north Idaho. A rich plume of Pacific moisture enhanced into heavy wet snow across the Cascades and eastern Washington. Outside of the Cascades the heaviest snow accumulations were noted just to the north of the track of the low pressure and was largely confined to a 50 mile wide area across the northern Columbia Basin and the southern portions of the northern Washington mountain zones. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist cold front passed through eastern Washington bringing mountain snow and valley rain to the region during the early morning hours of January 18th. A spotter near Metaline Falls reported 0.46 inches of moderate intensity rain during this event. Shortly after the rain ended a mudslide was reported from the area. Over the next few days mild temperatures melted low elevation snow pack and lead to saturated ground conditions triggering isolated mudslides and debris flows in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow was reported covering one lane of Highway 31 south of Metaline Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist cold front passed through eastern Washington bringing mountain snow and valley rain to the region during the early morning hours of January 18th. A spotter near Metaline Falls reported 0.46 inches of moderate intensity rain during this event. Shortly after the rain ended a mudslide was reported from the area. Over the next few days mild temperatures melted low elevation snow pack and lead to saturated ground conditions triggering isolated mudslides and debris flows in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide on State Route 209 near Colbert closed down one lane of the road until the highway department cleared it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of January 23rd a warm front formed over eastern Washington and North Idaho fed by a deep and favorably directed fetch of Pacific moisture. The front became stationary and produced a 24 to 36 hour period of light to moderate snow impacting the mountains and valleys of the Cascades and northeastern Washington. The Okanogan Valley also received heavy snow from this storm system. The Columbia Basin was in the warm sector of this storm system and received mostly rain with 2 to 5 inches of wet snow noted in the far western basin near the Cascades and on the Waterville Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Holden Village recorded 13.3 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the evening of the 28th of February and continuing through the 1st of March  a slow moving frontal band brought an extended period of wet snow to the Cascades, The Okanogan Highlands and the Northeast Mountains and the higher terrain above the western Columbia and the Okanogan River valleys. The accumulating snow was very elevation dependent with only a few hundred feet elevation making the difference between little or no accumulation and accumulations of upwards of a foot of very wet heavy snow. In the lower valleys mostly rain fell, and the saturated ground triggered a local debris flow near Omak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public 6 miles west of Orin reported 9 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time snow melt and moderate rain over the area created saturated soil conditions which caused a debris flow across the southbound lanes of State Route 21 near Curlew.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rock slide blocked the southbound lanes of Highway 21 about 9 miles north of Curlew.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front laying west to east over eastern Washington and north Idaho on February 17th developed into surface low pressure and finally an Arctic cold front which sagged southward through the region during the day of February 18th. A fetch of Pacific moisture fed into the surface low and enhanced into a period of moderate intensity snow during the day of February 17th and into the morning of the 18th. Heavy snow accumulations resulted over the Cascades and northeast Washington mountains.  This storm also brought lesser but still significant accumulations of snow to the Spokane area and the Palouse region before very dry Arctic air descending out of Canada behind the front effectively ended precipitation over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 8 miles north of Republic reported 5.3 inches of storm total new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved into the region during the night of March 23rd and the morning of the 24th. Many valley locations in the northeast mountains of Washington received 1 to 3 inches of snow from this late season storm, however there were a number of notably higher amounts in scattered pockets and valleys around the region. In particular, northern Ferry and northeast Okanogan Counties and southeastern Pend Oreille  and extreme northern Spokane County were hit harder by this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Wauconda reported 6.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance passing through the region on February 24th brought locally heavy snow accumulations to the Washington Palouse region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night of February 13th a surface low pressure formed over eastern Washington and tracked southeastward into central Idaho on February 14th before weakening. The western Columbia Basin received minor accumulations of snow which quickly melted during the day, however the rising terrain of the eastern basin including the Palouse region and the Spokane area as well as the northeastern mountains of Washington were north of the track and under the most intense precipitation from this storm. Widespread heavy snow accumulations resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP Observer at Boundary Dam recorded 4.4 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning during the evening of the 28th of February and continuing through the 2nd of March  a slow moving frontal band brought an extended period of wet snow to the Cascades, The Okanogan Highlands and the Northeast Mountains and the higher terrain of the western Columbia and Okanogan River valleys. The accumulating snow was very elevation dependent with only a few hundred feet elevation making the difference between little or no accumulation and accumulations of upwards of a foot of very wet heavy snow. The East Slopes of the Cascades began to accumulate snow during the evening of the last day of February, with the area of precipitation spreading eastward during the first two days of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at Holden Village recorded 16.0 inches of new snow accumulation from the evening of February 28th through the morning of March 1st when precipitation tapered off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of disturbances brought snow to the region late Friday February 23rd and on Saturday February 24th. The most significant snow fall occurred in the northern Blue mountains.

In the lower valleys mostly rain fell, and the saturated ground triggered a local debris flow near Omak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer 4 miles north of Republic reported 16.5 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surf was relatively benign for the time of year, as seas were generally around 5 ft with dominant wave period around 8 to 9 seconds. No one is sure quite what happened, but two people clamming in different locations on Washington Coast beaches in Pacific County were swept away by the surf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No one is quite sure what happened, but two people clamming in different locations on Washington Coast beaches in Pacific County were swept away by the surf within minutes of each other. One was clamming at Oysterville beach, the other was clamming near Warrenton Cannery Road in the community of North Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and progressive weather pattern continued through the first week in November as a strong westerly jet stream persisted over the Cascade Mountains. These stronger winds aloft occasionally surfaced especially in the overnight and morning hours into the  downslope lee valleys of the Cascades east slopes.  Toppled trees lead to scattered power outages in communities in these lower elevation valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chelan Public Utility Department reported power outages due to trees fouling power lines in the Brender Canyon area near Dryden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chelan County Public Utility Company reported a power outage on the Entiat River Road near Entiat caused by fallen trees in power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure system moving up from the south brought high winds across southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment reported sustained winds up to 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure system moving up from the south brought high winds across southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three Corner Rock RAWS reported sustained winds up to 55 mph with gusts up to 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the Cascades during the night of November 3rd and the morning of November 4th. Strong downsloping winds over the east slopes of the Cascades behind this front downed numerous trees in the Entiat, Leavenworth and Chelan areas with scattered power outages noted in the region. Higher elevation RAWs stations in the Cascades recorded wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph and these gusts apparently surfaced into the valleys immediately adjacent to the high terrain with a gust of 58 mph recorded at the Wenatchee airport.  As the cold front crossed the Columbia Basin widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wenatchee Airport ASOS station recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds damaged power lines in the Ski Hill area of Leavenworth leaving an estimated 650 customers without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked northeast into British Columbia. The associated cold front brought with it strong southerly winds to the south Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 67 mph.  Another weather station on Megler Bridge recorded sustained winds up to 43 mph.  Als, WSDOT reported a power line down on Highway 101, knocked down by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second week of April brought a period of wet weather to northeast Washington. Boundary Dam measured 1.79 inches of rain between April 11th and the 17th. Newport measured 1.6 inches during the same period and Mazama measured 1.01 inches. Holden Village recording 1.48 inches. All of this rain lead to saturated ground conditions by the beginning of the third week of April and set the stage for a rash of areal floods and debris flows in the mountainous areas of northeast Washington. Okanogan County was particularly hard hit with numerous road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areal flooding issues were noted across much of Okanogan County during the third week of April, including Green Lake Road closed due to flooding of Brown Lake over the roadway, North Star Road closed near Three Devils Road due to water and mud over the roadway, Windy Hill Road closed due to mud and water over the road and Loup Loup Canyon Road closed due to water over the road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salmon Creek Road was closed for three days due to mud covering the roadway. The mud came off of a burn scarred hillside and was 4 feet deep in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and rain created saturated soil conditions which lead to a road washout near Curlew in Ferry County. The road bed was undermined by the flood water causing the washout and collapse of a 20 foot section of the highway.  The road is a major thoroughfare through the region and was re-opened after 2 days of repair work.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road washout cut out and collapsed a 20 foot section of Highway 21 just north of Curlew.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of April 27th strong wind gusts to 50 mph downed trees and caused power outages in the Coles Corner and Lake Wenatchee area. The winds were likely the result of a cold front and terrain channeling between mountain ranges in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chelan County Public Utilities reported power outages in the Coles Corner and Lake Wenatchee area with trees down and blocking Cedar Brae Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system caused very windy conditions, with some locally strong winds, through the Columbia River Gorge, across the Columbia Basin and the Kittitas valley. Localized blowing dust also occurred in the Basin and in the Grande Ronde valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest sustained wind of 48 mph and highest gust of 59 mph occurred at the Bowers Field (KELN) on the north side of Ellensburg. Occurred around 600 PM PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of April 28th radar indicated scattered small convective showers over the Columbia Basin. A small funnel cloud was photographed by multiple citizens appending from a convective cell. The funnel cloud was brief and no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple members of the public reported and photographed a small funnel cloud near Ephrata.

This rain coupled with snow melt saturated the ground and promoted a number of road washouts and debris flows in Okanogan County on April 7th and 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock, dirt and debris slid off of a hillside and blocked Chesaw Road. Highway crews quickly opened up one lane of the road cleared the remaining lanes over the next week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of fog are common in the lower elevations of the Columbia Basin during the winter months. On the morning of November 29th dense fog formed along the Columbia River south of Mattawa. A driver attempted to pass another vehicle on a two lane road in this fog and ran into an oncoming semi truck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington State Patrol reported one fatality (indirect) when a motorist tried to pass another car on a two lane road during a period of dense fog. The car ran into an oncoming semi truck. The driver of the car was killed while the driver of the truck escaped injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool upper-level trough moved over the Pacific Northwest, generating enough instability for thunderstorms which produced gusty winds and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large gust of wind blew through the Harmony Sports Complex and tipped over a wooden equipment shed.  A man and young girl standing next to the shed were struck by the edge as it fell. The young girl was trapped under the shed, sustaining non-life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of November 22nd light freezing rain was noted in Grant County. A number of automobile accidents including a fatal accident occurred. A bus carrying members of the University of Washington Band slid off Interstate 90 and rolled but fortunately there were no serious injuries among the passengers. In a separate incident an SUV slid off a road and rolled resulting in one fatality and 3 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grant County Sheriff reported an SUV slid off of an icy road and rolled leading to one fatality and 3 injuries among the 4 occupants of the car. The Moses Lake ASOS reported freezing rain during the hours leading up to the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of November 22nd light freezing rain was noted in Grant County. A number of automobile accidents including a fatal accident occurred. A bus carrying members of the University of Washington Band slid off Interstate 90 and rolled but fortunately there were no serious injuries among the passengers. In a separate incident an SUV slid off a road and rolled resulting in one fatality and 3 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bus carrying 52 members of the University of Washington marching band encountered an icy road surface and slid off of Interstate-90 near George and rolled over resulting in minor injuries to the driver and 39 passengers. The Moses Lake Airport ASOS indicated intermittent light freezing rain during the evening of November 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Conrad Fire burned near Naches in Yakima county Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Conrad Fire in Yakima has burned 5,354 acres as of 10:00 a.m. on Tuesday the 3rd. Washington Department of Natural Resources officials said the fire is 85% contained. On Sunday evening, the 1st, nearly 50 homes in the area of Box Canyon and Conrad roads were evacuated. WADNR said no structures are threatened as of the 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ryegrass Coulee Fire burned in sage brush and light grass near Vantage in eastern Kittitas county Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ryegrass Coulee fire burning near Vantage is now 100 percent contained, days after the blaze first broke out late Monday night. The fire burned a total of 1,612 acres. It destroyed one outbuilding and damaged a barn, but no homes were lost. All evacuation orders for the town of Vantage were lifted around 7 p.m. Wednesday evening, the 11th.|The cause of the fire remains under investigation.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Miriam Fire was detected on July 30 at approximately 10:00 a.m. after lightning passed through the area on July 28. The fire is burning within the Goat Rocks Wilderness on the Naches Ranger District of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest 32 miles southwest of Naches, Washington. The fire is east of the White Pass Ski Area, south of Highway 12, and west of the Tieton River.||The fire is being managed to protect public and firefighter safety; the White Pass Ski Resort and surrounding area; recreational cabins, camps and resorts on Rimrock Lake; State Highway 12; Goat Rocks Wilderness; and fish and wildlife habitat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Locust Grove fire burned 2,275 acres in Benton county, Washington.  The fire occurred southwest of Kennewick, in the hills south of Badger Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Locust Grove fire was started by farming equipment around noon local daylight time southwest of Kennewick WA.  The fire was relatively slow moving, burning in grass and brush.  Homes south of Badger Rd where placed in Level 1 evacuation for a period of time.  State resources where activated at 1700 hours.  The fire was contained and in mop up by early evening.  No structures were ultimately affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The HWY 24 Command fire occurred at the east end of the Moxee Valley, approximately 17 miles east of Yakima.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The HWY 24 Command fire burned in grass and brush along and north of Washington State Highway 24 in northeast Yakima county.  The fire burned approximately 1,800 acres, and prompted road and local area closures.  Cause of the fire is still unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Boylston Fire burned over portions of eastern and southeastern Kittitas county in Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Boylston Fire near Kittitas burned 71,200 acres and was 80 percent contained by July 23rd. The fire started on Thursday the 19th in the median of I-90 east of the town of Kittitas. The fire spread east to the Columbia river and south into lands belonging to the Army's Yakima Training Center. The fire destroyed one residence and three other minor structures. As of the 23rd, Huntinger Road in Vantage is closed, while I-90 Westbound has reopened after being closed intermittently since Thursday. A single eastbound lane is now open and the Department of Transportation says traffic is being piloted through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This fire start around milepost 90 of Washington highway 14 in Klickitat county. The fire burned east along the Columbia River and the highway due to gusty west winds.  The fire consumed 14,500 acres of mostly grass and some sagebrush. Cause is under investigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt caused the Methow River to rise to a minor flood level and fluctuate slightly above and below the Flood Stage during the second and third weeks of May. No buildings or structures were threatened by this flood, however minor flooding of secondary roads and bottom lands near the river between Pateros and Mazama resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Methow River at Pateros recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 10.0 feet at 1:00 PM PSt on May 9th. The river crested at 10.7 feet at 7:15 AM on May 10th then receded below the Flood Stage at 7:30 PM on May 10th. The river rose again on May 16th and briefly touched the Flood Stage at 10.0 feet at 11:45 AM on May 16th before receding again.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt caused the Stehekin River to oscillate slightly above and below Flood Stage during the second and third week of May. No structures or buildings were threatened but secondary roads and bottom lands along the river were inundated from time to time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Stehekin River at Stehekin recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 24 feet at 5:15 PM PST on May 15th. The river fluctuated slightly above and below the Flood Stage due to the diurnal snow melt and run off cycle through May 17th, with a maximum crest of 24.4 feet at 11:00 PM on May 15th. The river dropped below the Flood Stage and continued to recede at 3:30 AM on May 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt caused the Similkameen River to achieve Moderate Flood Stage during the second and third week of May. No structures were flooded but a few homes in the river valley suffered yard inundation. The main impacts were extensive field and bottom land flooding in the river valley and the flooding of secondary roads near the river and along Palmer Lake, specifically the Loomis-Oroville Road and Chopaka Road.  Palmer Lake flooded as the river backed up along the outlet to the lake and into the lake itself.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Similkameen River at Nighthawk recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 14.0 feet at 8:00 PM PST on May 8th. The river continued to rise and passed through the Moderate Flood Stage of 15.0 Feet at 3:45 AM on May 10th. The river crested at 15.5 feet at 12:15 AM on May 11th before receding below the Flood Stage at 6:15 PM on May 12th. The river rose again and passed through Flood Stage at 9:00 PM PST on May 14th, with this second crest reaching the minor flood level of 14.8 feet at 2:00 AM on May 17th. The river then receded back below the Flood Stage at 5:00 AM on May 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt caused the Similkameen River to achieve Moderate Flood Stage during the second and third week of May. No structures were flooded but a few homes in the river valley suffered yard inundation. The main impacts were extensive field and bottom land flooding in the river valley and the flooding of secondary roads near the river and along Palmer Lake, specifically the Loomis-Oroville Road and Chopaka Road.  Palmer Lake flooded as the river backed up along the outlet to the lake and into the lake itself.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The flooding form the Similkameen River backed up into Palmer Lake during the high water period. This resulted in flooding along the Loomis Oroville Road along the lake shore and the inundation of Chopaka Road which crosses Palmer Creek which drains Palmer Lake into the Similkameen River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures in early May prompted accelerated snow melt in the mountains of British Columbia. Run off from this snow melt caused multiple areal flooding issues in Okanogan County during the second and third week of May. Lake Osoyoos began rising rapidly during the second week of May. Flooding occurred on properties along the lake shore during the second and third weeks of May as the water level peaked and stabilized. The water level began to recede on May 19th and continued to fall through the end of the month.||Salmon Creek near Conconully also rose out of it's banks due to snow melt in the higher elevations of the north Cascades. This river caused minor flooding problems in the town of Conconully and lead to a road closure along the southern stretch of river. ||The Chewuch River draining the north Cascades through Winthrop also rose out of it's banks causing minor flooding of roads and a few homes in the Winthrop area. ||All of these flooding issues along with the major flood along the Okanogan River prompted the county to be included in a State of Emergency Declaration issued by the Washington State Governor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On May 8th Conconully Lake reached the spillway level on the Conconully Dam due to heavy Spring runoff from the upper reaches of the Salmon River. The increased flow caused flooding along the river below the dam and resulted in the flooding and subsequent closure of Salmon Creek Road between Spring Coulee Road and Danker Cutoff Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Conconully reported that the North Fork of Salmon Creek had a rapid rise on May 6th, causing water to flow over the Broadway Street Bridge. Over a span of the next  two weeks, the North Fork rose and fell daily with the diurnal snow melt cycle, causing the water to occasionally flow over the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms formed during the evening of May 20th over the Palouse region of Washington and Idaho.  Heavy rain in the cores of these storms produced local flash flooding and debris flows in largely uninhabited rural areas but temporarily cut some highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a slow moving thunderstorm during the evening of May 20th caused Flash Flooding across Ladlow Butte Road near Garfield. A car was swept off the road and into a ditch. The car's occupants were rescued without injury by bystanders. Photos taken as the flood was abating showed mud and debris over a quarter mile stretch of roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt and a period of moderate rain during the second week of May created saturated soil conditions over Ferry County. A debris flow deposited rocks onto Highway 395 near Orient, which is a major highway route through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small debris flow deposited rock onto the southbound lane of Highway 395 near Orient. Traffic was delayed as the rock was removed.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Cascades, and also around Arlington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Paradise on Mt. Rainier got 12 inches in 8 hours, then another 21 inches in the following 23 hours.  The White Pass DOT site recorded 24 inches of snow in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The rise on Osoyoos Lake, which has no official Flood Stage, began to flood properties and seep into homes around the lake shore beginning on May 9th.  By the morning of the 10th  flood waters had submerged Deep Bay Park and were flooding the basements and threatening the first floors of homes along Blackler Road and the boat launch along Veranda Drive.  As the lake crested and the level stabilized between May 11th and May 19th extensive flooding of low lands along the lake was noted with Veterans Memorial Park completely inundated. Numerous lake shore homes suffered first floor flooding during this crest period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the east slopes of the Cascades and the Columbia Basin in a rich feed of wrap around moisture from an upper low over Oregon during the night of May 17th.  These storms dumped a general quarter to half inch of rain over most of the western basin, but individual cells locally produced higher amounts with Ephrata measuring 0.72 inches. Local flash flooding and debris flows were noted from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from heavy rain producing thunderstorms occurred in Ephrata during the late evening of May 17th. Basin Street, the main road through the downtown business district  was flooded with 4 to 8 inches of water with first floor damage to multiple businesses in the downtown area. A portion of the ceiling in the Department of Corrections building collapsed due to standing water on the roof.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms during the evening of May 17th resulted in a debris flow of rocks and mud onto the Malaga-Alcoa Highway betrween mileposts 10 and 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upriver Beacon Wildfire began from an unknown cause on July 17th near Spokane. The blaze which occurred on the urban/rural interface of Spokane triggered mandatory evacuation of hundreds of home owners while the fire was aggressively attacked by numerous firefighting resources. The fire was contained within 48 hours, but not before the fire burned 116 acres of timber as well as one residence and two outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home and two outbuildings were destroyed in the Upriver Beacon Fire near Spokane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Chelan Hills Wildfire began on July 27 from an unknown cause. The fire burned 1842 acres of grass and brush on slopes above the Columbia River near the town of Orondo. Four homes and several orchards were damaged and several outbuildings were destroyed. The fire was fully contained on August 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chelan Hills Wildfire damaged 4 homes in the Orondo area as well as destroying several outbuildings and damaging orchards and crops in the fire area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt in combination with recent rains across the region led the Columbia River to rise above flood stage near Vancouver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Columbia River at Vancouver crested several times above flood stage May 17 through May 19th, with the highest crest at 16.14 feet around 8:15 AM on May 17th. This is 0.14 foot above flood stage. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the Cascades during the night of November 3rd and the morning of November 4th. Strong downsloping winds over the east slopes of the Cascades behind this front downed numerous trees in the Entiat, Leavenworth and Chelan areas with scattered power outages noted in the region. Higher elevation RAWs stations in the Cascades recorded wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph and these gusts apparently surfaced into the valleys immediately adjacent to the high terrain with a gust of 58 mph recorded at the Wenatchee airport.  As the cold front crossed the Columbia Basin widespread wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 911 caller reported a utility trailer blown into a car in Wenatchee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wagon Wheel fire burned in grass, brush and scattered timber north of Benton City in Benton county Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wagon Wheel fire burned in mostly brush, grass and scattered timber, affecting approximately 7,500 acres.  It prompted level three evacuations for 50 homes in a rural area north Benton City for a few hours.  Ultimately no homes were lost and no injuries have been reported.  The fire did however burn a boat, four abandoned vehicles, and one bus.  Suspected cause is a squirrel interacting with a transformer on a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Run off from melting of near record snow pack in the British Columbia mountains promoted record flooding on the Kettle River during the second and third weeks of May. Bottom lands and parks along the river were extensively inundated as well as yards and outbuildings. Many homes near the river suffered basement flooding and a few homes received first floor flooding. ||Highway 21 near Danville, a major highway through this rural area, was closed due to the river flooding the road. Customs Road, running along the north side of the river was flooded in multiple locations, cutting off access to numerous residencies along the river.   Highway 395, another major thoroughfare along the river, was threatened with erosion and traffic was restricted in places. One bridge over the Kettle River suffered partial erosion of the abutment requiring extensive repairs. ||The swollen river cut off access to to every home along the river between Curlew and Toroda through flooding of access roads and driveways. Emergency officials evacuated some stranded residents with boats.||In Stevens County the river eroded a steep sandy bank leading to an outbuilding being swept into the river on May 13th. The associated home was also threatened. This house was saved after heavy equipment physically moved the house farther from the bank. ||The extent of the flooding prompted the Governor of Washington State to include Ferry County in a State of Emergency Declaration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Kettle River near Ferry recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 18.5 feet at 6:00 AM PST on May 5th. The river continued to rise and passed through the Moderate Flood Stage of 19.5 feet at 11:30 AM on May 7th, and through the Major Flood Stage of 20.5 feet at 6:00 PM PST on May 9th. The river crested at a new record flood level of 22.5 feet at 6:15 PM on May 10th. This level broke the previous record level of 21.2 feet set in 1948. The river then receded, passing down through the Major Flood Stage on May 11th, and below the Moderate Flood Stage on May 19th. The river finally receded below the Flood Stage at 1:30 AM PST on May 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An above average snow pack in the mountains drained by the Pend Oreille and Clark Fork River basins melted at an accelerated rate during the month of May. Run off from this snow melt caused the Pend Oreille River to achieve moderate flood level for an extended period of time from the middle of May through the first week of June. ||Numerous homes along the river particularly between the towns of Newport and Jared suffered flooding. Inundated yards, recreation sites and first floor flooding of homes and buildings extended as far north as Ione. Water covered portions of Deeter, Sandy Shores, McCloud Creek, Yergens, Bear Paw and Davis Roads, and inundated the Sandy Shores neighborhood near Deeter Road.||Due to the high flow, cold water and debris in the channel, the Pend Oreille Sheriff's office closed the river to all recreation through the end of the month. Pend Oreille County was included in a State of Emergency Declaration by the Washington State Governor for this flooding event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Flow Gage at Abeni Falls Dam which controls the flow of the Pend Oreille River surpassed the Flood Flow of 95,000 Cubic Feet per Second (CFS) at 2:30 PM PST on May 11th. The flow increased and passed through the Moderate Flood Flow of 115,000 CFS at 12:15 PM on May 18th. The flow continued to increase and peaked at the  Moderate Flood reading of 121,000 CFS at at 5:00 AM on May 27th. The flow continued to fluctuate between 118,000 CFS and 121,000 CFS through the end of May. The flow receded to below the Flood Flow on June 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring time mountain snow melt of heavy snow pack in the Canadian Rockies and the north Cascades brought major flooding to the Okanogan River from Oroville to it's mouth at the Columbia River. Numerous homes and businesses along and near the river suffered basement and first floor flooding in addition to massive inundation of crop lands and orchards in the river valley, with associated irrigation and farming support infrastructure damage and loss.||County and state officials mobilized emergency response crews to shore up levees protecting towns along the river and filled and placed over 200,000 sandbags.  Many of the towns along the river were protected by levees, and while there were no major levee failures, minor flooding of homes and buildings behind these dikes did occur through seepage and a few minor breaches. Most of the flooding of homes occurred near the river between the towns. ||Okanogan County was included in a State of Emergency Declaration issued by the Washington State Governor for this flooding event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS River Gage on the Okanogan River at Tonasket recorded a rise above the Flood Stage of 15.0 feet at 4:45 AM PST on May 5th. The river continued to rise, passing through the Moderate Flood Stage of 17.0 Feet at 11:45 PM on May 7th and through the Major Flood Stage of 18.0 feet at 9:15 AM on May 9th. The river crested twice, the first and highest crest of 19.7 feet at 4:30 PM PST on May 11th and the second crest of 19.3 feet at 10:30 AM on May 18th. The river then receded, passing below Major Flood Stage at 9:30 PM on May 20th and below the Moderate Flood Stage at noon PST on May 22nd. The river continued to slowly recede finally passing below the Flood Stage at 2:30 AM on May 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mile Marker 44 fire burned in tall grass west of Highway 97 in southern Yakima county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mile Marker 44 fire burned in tall grass west of U.S. Highway 97 in southern Yakima county Washington.  This fire affected approximately 3,000 acres of open and rugged terrain.  No known structures were threatened.  Location was roughly 17 miles southwest of Toppenish WA.  The cause of this fire is still under investigation.  Information from NIFC daily reports.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 62 mph.  Another weather station in Ocean Park recorded wind gusts up to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure east of the Cascades with a low pressure system approaching from the west created strong easterly winds through the Columbia River Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather station across the river at Corbett High School measured wind gusts up to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Pacific Northwest, with strong southerly winds ahead of the cold front along beaches and headlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather station on Megler Bridge recorded winds gusting up to 64 mph. Another weather station at Cape Disappointment recorded sustained winds up to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad low pressure system off the coast of Washington and Oregon destabilized the atmosphere enough to generate a severe thunderstorm along the South Washington Coast. Cape Disappointment saw high winds from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong stationary low pressure system off the British Columbia coast brought impressively high seas into the Washington and Oregon Coast. Wave heights up to 37 feet were recorded at buoys off the coast, with top one-tenth wave heights up to 45 feet. Damaging surf caused severe beach erosion and severly damaged a campground along the beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large waves pounded the surf zone and adjacent beach area near Cape Disappointment and Long Beach.  The large waves also caused coastal flooding and severely damaged 10 campsites at Cape Disappointment State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was brief high wind on Lopez Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lopez Island recorded 45 mph sustained wind at 157 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system approaching the coast brought high southerly winds to beaches and headlands ahead of the cold front as it moved onshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 69 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Miriam Fire was detected on July 30 at approximately 10:00 a.m. after lightning passed through the area on July 28. The fire is burning within the Goat Rocks Wilderness on the Naches Ranger District of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest 32 miles southwest of Naches, Washington. The fire is east of the White Pass Ski Area, south of Highway 12, and west of the Tieton River. |The fire is being managed to protect public and firefighter safety; the White Pass Ski Resort and surrounding area; recreational cabins, camps and resorts on Rimrock Lake; State Highway 12; Goat Rocks Wilderness; and fish and wildlife habitat.|Fire suppression efforts have tapered off and crews are now working on fire line repair and pulling equipment.|Firefighting cost was $14.2 million, according to the Northwest Interagency Coordination Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple large wildfires in the Cascades and the mountains of northeast Washington produced copious quantities of dense smoke through much of the month of August. The Methow Valley in particular was bracketed by two large fires, the McCleod fire to the north and the Crescent Mountain Fire to the south. Smoke from these fires produced nearly continuous Unhealthy air quality and occasionally Hazardous air quality in Twisp and Winthrop between August 3rd and August 27th, when a cold front passed through and ventilated the region. ||The communities of Omak, Tonasket, Republic and Colville also suffered long periods of Unhealthy air quality conditions from these fires and the Boyds Fire near Kettle Falls, as well as occasional smoke intrusion from large wildfires in British Columbia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor in Nespelem recorded periods of Unhealthy air quality due to smoke from area wildfires through much of August. Air quality deteriorated into the Hazardous range for much of August 19th, and fluctuated between Unhealthy and Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups until a frontal passage brought breezy conditions and some rain on August 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple large wildfires in the Cascades and the mountains of northeast Washington produced copious quantities of dense smoke through much of the month of August. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor in Inchelium recorded periods of Unhealthy air quality due to smoke from area wildfires through much of August. Air quality deteriorated into the Very Unhealthy and Hazardous range from August 19th through August 20th before marginally improving until a cold front passage on August 27th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The air quality sensor at Omak recorded Unhealthy air quality through most of the month of August. On August 19th and 20th air quality deteriorated into the Hazardous range before moderating back to Unhealthy until a cold front passage cleared out the smoke on August 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions over Eastern Washington during July and August created dry fuel conditions across the region. Scattered dry thunderstorms moving through the region  during the last week of July, caused a number of wildfires some of which grew large and long lasting requiring considerable state and federal resources to contain. These large fires were generally in unpopulated forest land with no structures or homes lost. Smoke from these fires and fires in surrounding states and provinces often severely degraded air quality across eastern Washington during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crescent Mountain Wildfire 20 miles southwest of Twisp was caused by lightning and first detected on July 29th. The fire grew through the month of August eventually consuming 52,609 acres of timber in the upper reaches of the Twisp River Valley before being contained in mid September.  The fire burned in unpopulated wilderness and no structures were lost. The cost of the resources to fight the fire was estimated at 39.4 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions over Eastern Washington during July and August created dry fuel conditions across the region. Scattered dry thunderstorms moving through the region  during the last week of July, caused a number of wildfires some of which grew large and long lasting requiring considerable state and federal resources to contain. These large fires were generally in unpopulated forest land with no structures or homes lost. Smoke from these fires and fires in surrounding states and provinces often severely degraded air quality across eastern Washington during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cougar Creek Wildfire 12 miles northwest of Ardenvoir was started by lightning in late July. During the month of August the fire grew and consumed 42,712 acres of timber and brush. While the fire burned in unpopulated forest land, the margins of the fire did threaten nearby homes at times, however no structures were lost. The firefighting resources necessary to bring the fire under control were estimated at 42.3 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple large wildfires in the Cascades and the mountains of northeast Washington produced copious quantities of dense smoke through much of the month of August. The Methow Valley in particular was bracketed by two large fires, the McCleod fire to the north and the Crescent Mountain Fire to the south. Smoke from these fires produced nearly continuous Unhealthy air quality and occasionally Hazardous air quality in Twisp and Winthrop between August 3rd and August 27th, when a cold front passed through and ventilated the region. ||The communities of Omak, Tonasket, Republic and Colville also suffered long periods of Unhealthy air quality conditions from these fires and the Boyds Fire near Kettle Falls, as well as occasional smoke intrusion from large wildfires in British Columbia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor in Republic recorded almost continuous Unhealthy air quality due to smoke from area wildfires through much of August. Air quality deteriorated into the Very Unhealthy and Hazardous range from August 19th through August 20th. Air Quality fluctuated between Unhealthy and Very Unhealthy until a cold front passage on August 27th ventilated the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This fire start around milepost 90 of Washington highway 14 in Klickitat county. The fire burned east along the Columbia River and the highway due to gusty west winds. The fire consumed 14,500 acres of mostly grass and some sagebrush. Cause is under investigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bofer Canyon fire affected areas along the south edge of the city of Kennewick and grass/brush lands south of Kennewick in Benton county Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bofer Canyon fire started along Interstate 82 and quickly jumped Bofer Canyon Rd due to breezy winds, burning through dry grass and brush.  The fire moved into the south edge of Kennewick, affecting the The Heights at Canyon Lakes and the Inspiration Heights subdivisions.  Five homes were lost and 4 more suffered heat damage.  Also 6 outbuildings were destroyed and two horses were killed.  About 361 Benton PUD customers lost power due to the fire damaging or destroying power poles and transformers.  The fire also moved towards the Finley area, but was stopped before doing damage.   Cause of the fire remains unknown at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The HWY 24 Command fire occurred at the east end of the Moxee Valley, approximately 17 miles east of Yakima.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The HWY 24 Command fire burned in grass and brush along and north of Washington State Highway 24 in northeast Yakima county. The fire burned approximately 1,800 acres, and prompted road and local area closures. Cause of the fire still unknown.  Fire was suppressed and 60 percent contained on 8/1/18 with no further updates after that date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Meninick Pass fire burned short grass and sagebrush in Yakima county, Washington.  The fire was located roughly 10 miles west of Toppenish and 5 miles west of White Swan.  This fire was started by lightning and burned a total of 5,537 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Miriam Fire was detected on July 30 at approximately 10:00 a.m. after lightning passed through the area on July 28. The fire is burning within the Goat Rocks Wilderness on the Naches Ranger District of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest 32 miles southwest of Naches, Washington. The fire is east of the White Pass Ski Area, south of Highway 12, and west of the Tieton River.||The fire is being managed to protect public and firefighter safety; the White Pass Ski Resort and surrounding area; recreational cabins, camps and resorts on Rimrock Lake; State Highway 12; Goat Rocks Wilderness; and fish and wildlife habitat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Inciweb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions over Eastern Washington during July and August created dry fuel conditions across the region. Scattered dry thunderstorms moving through the region  during the morning of August 11th triggered an estimated 20 new fire starts in the mountains north of Twisp.  Many of these small fires merged into on large conflagration lasting through the remainder of August and requiring considerable state and federal resources to contain. These fires were generally in unpopulated forest land with no structures or homes lost. Smoke from these fires and fires in surrounding states and provinces often severely degraded air quality across eastern Washington during the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McCleod Wildfire 10 miles north of Mazama was started by lightning and first reported on August 11th. The fire grew to 24,411 acres, consuming mostly timber in unpopulated  mountainous terrain before being largely contained by mid September. No structures were lost but some back country roads were closed. The cost of fighting the fire was calculated at 13.1 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Grass Valley Wildfire near Grand Coulee began on August 11th from an unknown cause and quickly spread on breezy winds to consume 74,835 acres of grass and scrub  land, threatening the nearby towns of Grand Coulee and Electric City by the afternoon of August 12th. The fire destroyed 5 outbuildings and damaged one home before it was contained. One firefighter suffered severe burns when fire behavior unexpectedly intensified during the first day of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A firefighter was injured when he was trapped by flames while fighting the Grass Valley Wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Angel Springs Wildfire began on August 2nd seven miles northeast of Davenport due to a farm machine fire. Gusty winds and low relative humidity caused the fire to spread,  eventually consuming 5046 acres of crops, timber and grassland. Two homes and numerous other structures and outbuildings were destroyed before the fire was contained on August 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Springs Wildfire destroyed 6 structures, at least two of them homes, and 7 outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Boyds Wildfire on the west side of Lake Roosevelt near Kettle Falls began on August 11th from an unknown cause and eventually consumed 4712 acres of timber and brush. The fire mostly consumed timber on the slopes west of Lake Roosevelt, however at lower elevations the fire closed the main highway along Lake Roosevelt and destroyed two homes and several outbuildings. The fire was largely contained by September 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington Dept. of Natural Resources reported two homes and several additional structures destroyed in the Boyds Wildfire. The cost of fire fighting resources involved in containing this fire was calculated at 12.6 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple large wildfires in the Cascades and the mountains of northeast Washington produced copious quantities of dense smoke through much of the month of August. The Methow Valley in particular was bracketed by two large fires, the McCleod fire to the north and the Crescent Mountain Fire to the south. Smoke from these fires produced nearly continuous Unhealthy air quality and occasionally Hazardous air quality in Twisp and Winthrop between August 3rd and August 27th, when a cold front passed through and ventilated the region. ||The communities of Omak, Tonasket, Republic and Colville also suffered long periods of Unhealthy air quality conditions from these fires and the Boyds Fire near Kettle Falls, as well as occasional smoke intrusion from large wildfires in British Columbia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An air quality sensor at Twisp recorded Unhealthy air quality from August 3rd through August 27th with occasional spikes into the Hazardous category. A cold front passage on August 27th brought breezy westerly winds which ventilated the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoons of June 21st and June 22nd an upper level trough interacted with a moist and unstable air mass producing numerous thunderstorms with heavy rain and hail over the northeast mountains of Washington. Brief torrential rain and small hail was common in the cores of these storms. At least one of these storms produced large hail and another storm produced an apparent wet micro burst which toppled two trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported one inch diameter hail from a thunderstorm near Colville. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late night and early morning hours of June 25th a strong cold front transited eastern Washington. The front exploited mid level instability and a moist air mass as it crossed the Cascades and broke out into eastern Washington. A line of thunderstorms developed between midnight and 4 AM PST and transited west to east through the Cascades and the mountains of northeastern Washington. Many of these storms were severe with damaging outflow winds blowing down trees, damaging structures and producing power outages in the Cascades and the Okanogan Highlands. Several fires were started by lightning strikes as well.  The line weakened as it moved through north Idaho with no further damage reports, and passed into Montana by 7 AM PST, ending further thunderstorm activity.||Many people confirmed this event's damage with pictures of buildings and hundreds of trees lost in small, localized areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Aeneas RAWS station reported southwest winds of 37 mph sustained with a gust of 64 mph as a cold front passed through the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Easterday fire burned in southeast Benton county Washington. The fire affected mainly grass, scrub brush and agricultural land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire crews responded to a brush fire near Easterday Farms, just south of Nine Canyon Road on Friday, June 22nd. It spread into Switzler Canyon which could not be directly accessed due to poor terrain. Late in the afternoon the fire jumped a dozer line requiring a request for more responders. The fire was stopped towards midnight at around 1,000 acres. Mop up continued into the 23rd. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Montecito Fire affected a portion of Benton county Washington near Prosser in late June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montecito Fire started on June 27, 2018, at approximately 7:30 p.m.  This fire is estimated at 2,500 acres, and is threatening 50 homes, crops, and infrastructure in the area.  Level 2 evacuation orders are in effect at this time.  The cause of the fire is under investigation.  Fire was contained by early afternoon June 28 with all evacuation orders lowered.  Final acreage burned was 1,877 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front laying west to east over eastern Washington and north Idaho on February 17th developed into surface low pressure and finally an Arctic cold front which sagged southward through the region during the day of February 18th. A fetch of Pacific moisture fed into the surface low and enhanced into a period of moderate intensity snow during the day of February 17th and into the morning of the 18th. Heavy snow accumulations resulted over the Cascades and northeast Washington mountains.  This storm also brought lesser but still significant accumulations of snow to the Spokane area and the Palouse region before very dry Arctic air descending out of Canada behind the front effectively ended precipitation over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer at Plain recorded 12.9 inches of new snow accumulation from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance passing through the region on February 24th brought locally heavy snow accumulations to the Washington Palouse region. A second subsequent storm right on it's heels on February 25th brought little additional snow but did generate breezy conditions which caused locally severe drifting and blowing of the previous day's snow on the exposed terrain  of the Palouse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Pullman reported 8.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day of December 11th a moist warm front developed over eastern Washington. The mountains and valleys of northeast Washington received heavy snow from this system, although the southern valleys switched over to rain late in the day. This storm brought light to moderate accumulations of heavy wet snow to the northern Columbia Basin as well but amounts were limited to terrain above 2000 feet elevation where 1 to locally 3 inches fell before snow changed over to rain in the evening as the warm front passed through. This change-over created very hazardous road conditions in the northern Columbia Basin, claiming the life of one motorist.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Northport reported 4.0 inches of snow accumulation from this storm.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow associated with an atmospheric river fell in the Washington Cascades with 11 inches at Snoqualmie Pass and 8 inches at Hyak. Interstate 90 was closed from North Bend to Ellensburg for 3 hours due to multiple spin-outs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven inches of snow fell at Snoqualmie Pass. Interstate 90 was closed for 3 hours due to multiple spin-outs on the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low passed well to the north, bringing high wind to the northwest interior and coast.  The high wind extended south into the Seattle region.  The wind was not as strong in the Seattle area as it was on December 14--only 30,000 Seattle City Light customers were without power at the storm's peak.  However, it was the strongest storm of the season so far in the northwest interior, where power outages were widespread, and some of the outages lasted nearly a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS on Whidbey Island (KNUW) recorded a 62 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of heavy rain, a cold unstable air mass resulted in isolated strong thunderstorms during the afternoon of December 18, 2018. The strongest storm developed over Kitsap County, WA, ultimately producing an EF-2 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1350PST, a tornado touched down near Port Orchard, WA. This tornado continued on the ground in a northeast direction for approximately 5 minutes. Significant structure damage was evident in the damage path, including one home where the roof was completely removed. Ultimately, 300 homes and businesses were impacted by the tornado. It resulted in 25 red-tagged (uninhabitable), and 20 yellow-tagged (habitable at own risk) homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through eastern Washington from the northwest during the afternoon and evening of  October 2nd. Winds behind this front channeled down the Okanogan Valley producing local gusts of up to 70 mph. In the Northeast Mountains the Colville River Valley which also runs north to south apparently promoted channeling of these post frontal winds, and resulted in toppled trees and power lines and some roof damage in and near Colville. In the Columbia Basin wind gusts were generally in the 40 to 50 mph range and while no damage was reported, areas of blowing dust reduced visibility to 3 to 5 miles in many locations with isolated pockets of visibility less than 1 mile in blowing dust during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oroville RAWS remote weather station near Oroville recorded a wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of October 12th a line of showers and thunderstorms produced damaging local outflow winds near the town of Diamond Lake in the northeast mountains of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 4 trees uprooted and a few power poles snapped with power outages as a result of strong local outflow winds from a nearby thunderstorm. Time is estimated from radar data.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Cascades and Olympics.  This was the first event of the season, so warnings were issued even though warning criteria were not necessarily forecast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snoqualmie Pass reported 9 inches of snow.  Stevens Pass reported 19 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE WASHINGTON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four inches of slushy snow accumulation fell at Hyak, just below Snoqualmie pass. This was the first significant snow for the pass this winter and multiple spin-outs resulted in Interstate 90 being closed in both directions for 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 90 over Snoqualmie Pass was closed for 4 hours after multiple spin-outs due to 4 inches of wet and slushy snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved onto the coast, bringing high winds, mainly to beaches and headlands along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Megler Bridge measured wind gusts up to 78 mph. Another weather station on Cape Disappointment measured sustained winds up to 54 mph, with gusts up to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast toward Victoria Island. The trailing cold front moved onto the coast, bringing strong southerly winds ahead of the front to the coast and the Willapa Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abernathy RAWS recorded wind gusts up to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through. This generated strong winds across southwest Washington, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Grays river. Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 68 mph. Another weather station on Megler Bridge recorded wind gusts up to 65 mph. Toke Point RAWS also recorded sustained winds up to 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through. This generated strong winds across southwest Washington, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Grays river. Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abernathy RAWS recorded wind gusts up to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system over the Gulf of Alaska brought a strong cold front through. This generated strong winds across southwest Washington, and also brought heavy rain which caused flooding on the Grays river. Large seas also caused damage in spots along beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays River near Rosburg crested at 13.2 feet around 11:30 AM, which is 1.2 feet above flood stage. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast toward Victoria Island. The trailing cold front moved onto the coast, bringing strong southerly winds ahead of the front to the coast and the Willapa Hills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Cape Disappointment recorded wind gusts up to 73 mph. Another weather station just south on Megler Bridge recorded wind gusts up to 75 mph.  Other weather stations in the area recorded wind gusts up to around 64 to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving into western Canada brought strong winds to the south Washington coast. As the cold front approached the coast, southerly winds picked up, gusting to 60 to 65 mph before turning westerly and decreasing behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather station at Cape Disappointment measured wind gusts up to 66 mph.  Another weather station on Megler Bridge recorded sustained winds up to 47 mph.
